steps.text: Perform studies to determine whether recoveries and responsiveness to interferences of QC materials are representative of the true analyte.
Ensure that control material does not interfere with the detection of the true analyte at low concentration.
Perform single-laboratory testing in the matrix types for which the method will be used for Tier 1 validation.
Perform a three-laboratory multilaboratory study using the matrix in which the method will be used for Tier 2 validation.
Perform a nine-laboratory multilaboratory study on nine matrix types for Tier 3 validation.
Prepare the diluted test chemical from a reagent-grade product per the preparation sheets provided by the Study Director.
Verify available chlorine for diluted samples using HACH Test Kits.
Initiate a new stock culture of Bacillus subtilis (ATCC # 19659).
Produce spores for use in the TSM per the provided method.
Inoculate carriers from one spore preparation per laboratory, ensuring enough carriers are inoculated to perform the entire study.
Test for inhibitory residues on glassware periodically.
Add 0.25 g of manganese sulfate to 500 mL water.
Filter sterilize the manganese sulfate monohydrate solution.
Standardize and adjust dilute hydrochloric acid to 2.5M as in 936.15.
Initiate B. subtilis culture (e.g., use nutrient broth to rehydrate a lyophilized culture, and incubate the broth culture for 24 ± 2 hours at 36 ± 1ºC prior to streak inoculation).
Streak inoculate a set (e.g., six) nutrient agar slopes and incubate 24 ± 2 hours at 36 ± 1ºC.
Perform purity and identification confirmation testing for QC (e.g., colony morphology on TSA, Gram stain, or use of other identification systems).
Following incubation, store at 2-5ºC.
Maintain stock culture on nutrient agar slants by monthly (30 ± 2 days) transfers.
Using growth from a stock culture tube, inoculate 10 mL tubes of nutrient broth and incubate tubes 24 ± 2 hr on an orbital shaker at approximately 150 rpm at 36 ± 1ºC.
Use this culture to inoculate amended nutrient agar plates.
Inoculate each plate with 500 µl of broth culture and spread the inoculum with a sterile bent glass rod or suitable spreading device.
Wrap each plate with parafilm or place in plastic bags.
Incubate plates inverted for 12-14 days at 36 ± 1ºC.
Following incubation, harvest the spores by adding 10 mL cold sterile water to each plate.
Using a spreader (e.g., bent glass rod), remove growth from plates and pipet suspensions into 15 mL sterile conical tubes.
Centrifuge tubes at 5000 rpm for approximately 10 min at room temperature.
Remove and discard supernatant.
Resuspend pellet.
Label fraction A, B and C microcentrifuge tubes with fraction letter (e.g. A, B, or C) and carrier number (e.g., 1, 2, 3, etc.).

Prepare fraction B and fraction C microcentrifuge tubes: Add 400 μL of ice-cold sterile water to fraction B tubes, add 400 μL of ice-cold LB broth to fraction C tubes, and store these tubes in a refrigerator (2-5°C) until ready for use.
Label dilution microcentrifuge tubes (serial dilution blanks) with fraction letter (e.g. A, B, or C), carrier number (e.g. 1, 2, 3, etc.), and dilution (e.g., 10-1, 10-2, 10-3, etc.).
Prepare serial dilution blanks for A, B, and C fractions for all test carriers.
Determine the intended use of the PCR method.
Choose the genomic region that will be targeted by the assay.
Predict the specificity and sensitivity of the primers and/or probe for the intended genetic target using available sequence databases and computer software.
Design controls to estimate sample preparation efficiency as well as PCR assay inhibition.
Select a particular IAC strategy, considering the advantages, drawbacks, and intended use of the PCR method.
Add an amplification control sequence to each reaction.
Perform mixture studies to measure the ability of the PCR method to obtain reliable results from mixed source samples.
Test a series of serial dilutions of known concentrations or copy numbers to construct a standard curve.
Generate a linear plot of CT vs. the logarithm of the target copy number.

Calculate ROQ by determining the difference between upper and lower (LOQ) bound concentrations where quantitative measurements are linear and within acceptable levels of precision, accuracy, and specificity.
Calculate amplification efficiency from the slope of a qPCR standard curve.

preconditions.text: QC materials (matrix spike or internal control materials) are utilized in the method.
An analysis of formulation chemistry must be performed on each lot by OPP chemists to confirm the percent active ingredient in advance of testing.
If using an existing stock culture, ensure it meets quality control standards and obtain Study Director approval.
Carrier counts and HCl resistance must be determined by the vendor and meet AOAC method specifications in advance of shipping the carriers.
Obtain Bacillus subtilis (ATCC No. 19659) directly from a commercial supplier.
Growth from a stock culture tube is available.
Prior to Testing (i.e. day before test)
The PCR method is designed to detect target sequences in complex mixtures of heterologous nucleic acid preparations that can significantly vary in composition and amount.
Prior to pursuing method validation, knowledge of the target performance standard is essential.

constraints.text: Validation must be performed on a minimum of nine matrix types in each sample for which the method will be applied.
No organic burden will be added to the spore inoculum.
Personal protective clothing or devices are recommended during the handling of these items for purpose of activation, dilution, or efficacy testing.
A chemical fume hood or other containment equipment may be employed when appropriate during performing tasks with concentrated products.
The study analyst may wish to consult the Material Safety Data Sheet for the specific product/active ingredient to determine best course of action.
Detergents used in washing glassware may leave residues which are bacteriostatic.
Reagent-grade water should be free of substances that interfere with analytical methods.
Carriers exposed to the disinfectant(s) and water control will be tested in triplicate.
It is recommended that no more than three disinfectants plus the water control be tested during the same test period.
The amplification control signal should always be produced even when there is no target nucleic acid present.
PCR inhibition can completely prevent the amplification of target nucleic acids or reduce sensitivity, resulting in false negatives or incorrect real-time quantitative PCR measurements.
Mixture ratios should represent the range of conditions that may be encountered when implementing the method (SWGDAM 2004).
Use five to eight dilutions (e.g., 10-fold) bracketing the range of concentrations for which the PCR method will be used.
Scientists performing the tests must be trained in the conduct of the method.
Following the method exactly is crucial for proficiency and validity of test results.
